title: Built-in Collections
description: Python's optimized built-in collections: lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries — featuring all operations, helper methods, time complexities, and comprehensions.

sections.title: List Operations & Complexity
sections.description: Lists are mutable, dynamic arrays. Essential operations include Indexing, Slicing, Insertion (append/insert), Deletion (pop/remove), Searching (index/in), and Sorting.
sections.codeBlock:
lst = [10, 20, 30]

# 1. Access & Indexing - O(1)
print(lst[0])          # 10

# 2. Insertion
lst.append(40)         # O(1) amortized - Adds at end: [10, 20, 30, 40]
lst.insert(1, 15)      # O(N) - Shifts elements: [10, 15, 20, 30, 40]

# 3. Deletion
last_val = lst.pop()   # O(1) - Removes from end: 40
mid_val = lst.pop(1)   # O(N) - Removes index 1 and shifts: 15
lst.remove(20)         # O(N) - Searches and removes value 20

# 4. Searching & Membership
present = 30 in lst    # O(N) linear search
idx = lst.index(30)    # O(N) returns index of value 30

# 5. Sorting & Reversal
lst.sort()             # O(N log N) - Timsort algorithm in-place
lst.reverse()          # O(N) - Inverts order in-place
print(lst)             # [30, 10]

sections.title: Tuple Operations & Complexity
sections.description: Tuples are immutable ordered records. Because they are immutable, they do not support insertion or deletion operations, but support O(1) indexing, unpacking, and hashing.
sections.codeBlock:
tup = (1, 2, 3)

# 1. Indexing & Slicing - O(1)
print(tup[1])          # 2
print(tup[0:2])        # (1, 2)

# 2. Unpacking - O(N)
x, y, z = tup
print(x, y, z)         # 1 2 3

# 3. Membership & Counting
print(2 in tup)        # O(N) - True
print(tup.count(3))    # O(N) - 1 (occurrences of 3)
print(tup.index(2))    # O(N) - 1 (index of value 2)

sections.title: Set Operations & Complexity
sections.description: Sets represent unordered collections of unique elements. They are built on hash tables, providing O(1) average time complexities for insertion, deletion, and membership checks.

sections.title: Dictionary Operations & Complexity
sections.description: Dictionaries store key-value pairs. They provide O(1) average time complexities for key lookup, insertion, updating, and deletion.
sections.codeBlock:
d = {"name": "Alice", "role": "admin"}

# 1. Insertion & Update - O(1) average
d["email"] = "alice@example.com"
d["role"] = "superuser"

# 2. Safe Access - O(1) average
print(d.get("age", 25))# 25 (returns fallback if key is missing)

# 3. Deletion - O(1) average
val = d.pop("role")    # Removes and returns "superuser"
print(d)               # {'name': 'Alice', 'email': 'alice@example.com'}

# 4. Iteration
for k, v in d.items(): # O(N) traversal
    print(k, v)

details:
Memory Management: Python lists are implemented as variable-length arrays of pointers to objects (equivalent to std::vector<PyObject*> in C++). Resizing follows an over-allocation pattern to achieve amortized O(1) appends, but inserting at the beginning forces a shift of all subsequent elements, taking O(N) time. Tuples are immutable and are allocated in a single block of memory, which makes them lighter and faster to construct than lists.

Hash Table Implementations: Both set and dict are built on highly optimized hash tables. Dictionaries use a dense array of entries and an index table to preserve insertion order (since Python 3.6/3.7) while maintaining O(1) average lookup times. Keys must be hashable (immutable objects like strings, numbers, or tuples containing only immutable types). Sets are essentially dictionaries without values, utilizing identical open addressing hashing mechanics to ensure quick element checking.
